old_claim.note: Military Signal Flags provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped warning network used deliberately lit fires, smoke by day, and flame by night, so controlled fire is the required signal medium.

source_shape.source_locator: Historic England says beacons were fires deliberately lit to give warning by smoke in daylight and flame at night, and describes bonfires, pitch barrels, and fire baskets.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source defines medieval beacons as deliberately lit fire signals using smoke and flame for warning.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because fire and smoke are the signal medium, controlled fire is a required method dependency. Flag signalling is a separate visual-signalling practice and is not evidenced as the prerequisite for this beacon-network scope.

ontology_before: Castle Signal Beacons depended on Military Signal Flags, mixing fire-beacon warning networks with flag and banner command signaling.
ontology_after: Formalized Medieval Beacon Networks depend on controlled fire as the physical signaling medium and exclude military flags as a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: castle_signal_beacons now depends on fire_control and no longer directly depends on military_signal_flags.
would_reject_if: The node were broadened back to all visual military signalling including flags and banners.
A source showed the formalized medieval beacon system depended on flag signalling rather than fire and smoke.
The fire_control node were later narrowed to exclude maintained signal fires, smoke, or night flame.
short_reason: Medieval beacon networks used fire and smoke, not flag signalling, as the signal medium.
